topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias traced the embossed lettering on the brittle parchment, the ink seeming to mock him with its polished finality. He had spent the last week in a haze of frantic cross-referencing, the crisp edges of the documents blurring under the strain of his mounting unease. When the final page revealed the dates, the locations, the very cadence of his supposed life—all meticulously crafted by someone else—a cold, heavy weight settled in his chest. He leaned back in the worn leather chair, the silence of his study amplifying the frantic thumping in his ears. His hands, usually steady when composing, now trembled slightly as he smoothed a passage detailing a childhood memory he distinctly recalled as being entirely his own. A slow, deep sigh escaped him, a sound heavy with unspoken acknowledgment of a profound betrayal. He stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the room, his gaze darting from the window to the stack of papers, a palpable sense of disorientation washing over him.

[story 2]

The discovery hit Clara like a physical blow, stealing the air from her lungs. She stared at the meticulously arranged timeline, the narrative of her existence laid bare, every triumph and every stumble framed by an author she never met. A tightness constricted her throat, making swallowing a conscious, difficult act. She crumpled a corner of the document, the sound sharp in the quiet apartment. "This isn't right," she whispered, the sound thin and reedy. Her fingers clenched into fists, knuckles whitening against the paper. She sank onto the floor, the cool wood pressing against her knees. For a long time, she didn't move, just absorbing the sheer audacity of the deception. When she finally forced herself to look up, her eyes were glassy, reflecting a deep, internal ache. She began to meticulously tear the pages, a desperate attempt to erase the imposed story, her movements jerky and uncontrolled.

[story 3]

Marcus sat at the kitchen table, the overhead light casting harsh shadows across the open file. He reread the biography, his breath catching in shallow gasps. The details were so intimate, so perfectly calibrated to his unspoken aspirations, yet none of the texture felt authentic. A strange hollowness opened up inside him, a vacuum where certainty used to reside. He picked up a pen, his grip surprisingly weak, and began to sketch lines over the printed text, not correcting it, but simply overlaying his own messy, unedited thoughts onto the fabricated structure. He pressed the pen hard, the tip digging into the paper. A low groan escaped him, a sound of utter defeat. He dropped the pen onto the counter, his shoulders slumping forward, the weight of the manufactured life pressing down on him until he felt physically unable to lift his head.
